critic_raw:
1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE EXPLICITLY STATED. The design explicitly states that 'all transformers (audio MFCC extraction parameters, thermal image normalization statistics, video frame standardization, and any scaling or imputation) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' This is a direct admission of data leakage. MFCC normalization statistics, thermal pixel intensity statistics, and video standardization parameters computed on test data contaminate preprocessing decisions. The defense that this 'guarantees more stable estimates' does not override the fundamental problem: test-set distributional information is now baked into every transformed observation. For failure detection where the positive class is rare, even small systematic shifts in normalization can inflate apparent generalization.

2. MACHINE-LEVEL TEMPORAL LEAKAGE FROM RANDOM SPLITTING. The split is described as stratified random sampling with machine-level stratification but randomized across months. Sensor time-series data has strong temporal autocorrelation — a window recorded at time T is highly correlated with windows at T-1 and T+1 from the same machine. Randomly assigning these correlated windows across train/test creates a situation where test windows are near-duplicates of training windows in feature space. The reported performance will be inflated relative to what the model would achieve on genuinely future, unseen data. A temporal split (train on months 1-8, validate 9-10, test 11-12) is the methodologically correct approach for time-series predictive maintenance.

3. LABEL ASYMMETRY BETWEEN CLASSES. Positive labels require a technician-documented repair within 48 hours of the sensor anomaly, but negative labels are any 7-day windows with no repairs. The 48-hour failure window and 7-day normal window are not symmetric. Borderline periods — sensor anomalies that led to repair on day 3 or day 4 — are excluded for cleanliness, but their exclusion rate is not quantified. If borderline periods constitute 10-20% of ambiguous cases, the resulting model trains on a simplified distribution that does not reflect deployment reality where these ambiguous cases will arrive without exclusion.

4. BASELINE COMPARISON IS NOT THE CLAIMED HYPOTHESIS. The hypothesis claims CNN on multi-modal sensor data versus decision trees, but the actual baseline implemented is a Random Forest with 100 trees and max_depth=15. Random Forests and decision trees are not the same model class. A single decision tree would be appropriate for the stated hypothesis; a Random Forest is an ensemble that frequently achieves near-neural-network performance on tabular data and is a substantially stronger competitor. The claimed hypothesis about CNN vs. decision trees is not being tested.

5. MACRO F1 OBSCURES OPERATIONALLY CRITICAL ASYMMETRY. The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 which weights both classes equally despite the design acknowledging they have asymmetric costs (false negatives = missed failures = unplanned downtime; false positives = unnecessary maintenance disruption). Equal-weight macro F1 cannot distinguish a model that catches every failure but floods maintenance with false alarms from one that is conservative. The operational hypothesis requires an asymmetric metric or explicit operating point selection, neither of which drives model selection here.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset before splitting constitutes explicit data leakage
Stratified random split on time-series data creates temporal leakage via autocorrelated windows
Asymmetric label windows (48-hour failure vs 7-day normal) with unquantified exclusion rate
Baseline is Random Forest not decision tree as stated in the hypothesis
Macro F1 as primary metric ignores the asymmetric operational costs of false negatives vs false positives
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset before splitting constitutes explicit data leakage
Stratified random split on time-series data creates temporal leakage via autocorrelated windows
Asymmetric label windows (48-hour failure vs 7-day normal) with unquantified exclusion rate
Baseline is Random Forest not decision tree as stated in the hypothesis
Macro F1 as primary metric ignores the asymmetric operational costs of false negatives vs false positives

critic_raw:
1. PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE VIA IMAGE-LEVEL RANDOM SPLIT. The split is performed at the image level using stratified random sampling, not at the patient level. Diabetic retinopathy datasets routinely contain multiple images per patient (bilateral imaging, follow-up visits). Random image-level splitting will place images from the same patient in both training and test sets. The model then partially memorizes patient-level features (fundus vessel patterns are highly individual-specific) rather than learning generalizable retinopathy features. Reported AUROC will be inflated relative to deployment performance on new patients.

2. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE ADMITTED. The design states 'all image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation.' Per-channel normalization statistics computed on the full dataset, including test images, constitute leakage. While normalization statistics tend to be relatively stable in imaging datasets, the explicit rationale ('robust and stable estimation') reveals this was a deliberate choice rather than an oversight, and it contradicts the stated principle of holding out the test set.

3. BASELINE IS THE GOLD STANDARD BEING REPLACED. The baseline is described as 'the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally.' This is not a model — it is the human annotation that produced the labels. Comparing a CNN against the very experts who labeled the data cannot produce an unbiased estimate of CNN advantage; the CNN is optimized against these labels while the baseline is evaluated as-is without any threshold tuning or optimization. Any CNN performance above the grading panel's self-agreement rate is partly an artifact of optimizing to that specific label set, not evidence of superior clinical judgment.

4. SEVERITY-ORDERED CLASSES REQUIRE ORDINAL METRICS. The ETDRS scale is ordinal: no retinopathy < mild < moderate < severe. Using macro-averaged AUROC with one-vs-rest treats all misclassification errors as equivalent. Misclassifying severe as no-retinopathy is clinically much worse than misclassifying mild as moderate, yet both contribute identically to macro AUROC. The design makes no mention of ordinal loss or weighted evaluation that reflects the clinical severity ordering.

5. 5000 IMAGES ACROSS FOUR CLASSES WITH HEAVY IMBALANCE IS INSUFFICIENT FOR ROBUST DEEP LEARNING. The severe class has approximately 250 images. Fine-tuning EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50 on 250 severe examples, even with augmentation, is severely underpowered for a model with millions of parameters. Early stopping on validation AUROC with 37-38 severe images in the validation set (15% of 250) means the stopping criterion is extremely noisy. The design does not address transfer learning depth, layer freezing strategy, or any explicit technique to handle the minority class beyond standard augmentation.
all_issues_raised: Image-level split instead of patient-level split causes patient leakage into training and test sets
Preprocessing normalization parameters fit on full dataset including test images
Baseline is the same expert panel that created the labels, making comparison circular
Macro AUROC with one-vs-rest ignores ordinal severity structure of ETDRS classes
Severe class has only ~250 images, insufficient for reliable fine-tuning of deep CNN
all_issues_adjudicated: Image-level split instead of patient-level split causes patient leakage into training and test sets
Preprocessing normalization parameters fit on full dataset including test images
Baseline is the same expert panel that created the labels, making comparison circular
Macro AUROC with one-vs-rest ignores ordinal severity structure of ETDRS classes
Severe class has only ~250 images, insufficient for reliable fine-tuning of deep CNN

critic_raw:
1. RANDOM STRATIFIED SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL HOSPITAL DATA IGNORES TEMPORAL AUTOCORRELATION. The design uses stratified random sampling across all 60 months. Hospital EHR data has strong temporal dependencies: patient census patterns, seasonal admission fluctuations, institutional practice changes, coding practice drift, and disease prevalence changes over 5 years. A random split places observations from the same time periods in both train and test, allowing the model to use temporally correlated context as a signal. A model evaluated this way will generalize poorly to future patients, which is the deployment scenario. The design briefly mentions temporal stratification in the confound controls section but the primary split is randomized.

2. MULTI-TASK AUXILIARY TARGET IS NOT AN INDEPENDENT SIGNAL. The auxiliary task is length-of-stay regression, but LOS is determined at discharge — the same time the readmission label is determined. LOS is itself a strong predictor of readmission: longer stays indicate more severe disease, higher comorbidity burden, and higher readmission risk. The shared encoder trained jointly on readmission and LOS will learn to use LOS-predictive signals as a readmission shortcut. This is not regularization in the conventional sense; it is training the shared encoder to co-optimize on two correlated targets where one (LOS prediction) partially encodes the answer to the other (readmission). The multi-task advantage claimed is confounded by this correlation.

3. CLINICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS ON DISCHARGE NOTES ARE HIGH-RISK FOR LABEL LEAKAGE. Discharge summaries contain the disposition plan, post-discharge follow-up instructions, and in some systems, explicit risk flagging language. Clinician-written discharge summaries at high-risk institutions may contain explicit phrases about readmission risk. The design states 'no future information is included' but discharge summary content written at discharge often reflects the clinician's implicit readmission risk assessment, encoding the label as a latent signal in the text. This is not leakage in a technical sense but it means the NLP model is partly recovering clinician risk judgments, not predicting from raw clinical data.

4. BASELINE LOGISTIC REGRESSION USES STRUCTURED FEATURES ONLY WHILE DEEP MODEL GETS BOTH STRUCTURED AND CLINICAL BERT. This is an unequal comparison. The baseline receives demographics, Charlson index, admission type, diagnosis codes, and utilization history. The multi-task model receives all of these plus TF-IDF bag-of-words and Clinical BERT embeddings from all clinical notes. Any performance improvement could be entirely attributable to the additional text modality rather than the multi-task architecture. A fair comparison would include a single-task deep model with the same feature set.

5. AUROC AS PRIMARY METRIC MISALIGNS WITH STATED STAKEHOLDER OBJECTIVE. The design states the stakeholder priority is 'minimizing both false positives and false negatives.' AUROC measures ranking ability across all thresholds and does not capture operating-point performance directly. The design simultaneously reports sensitivity at a clinically-calibrated threshold selected on validation data, which is the operationally meaningful metric. Using AUROC as primary but threshold-based sensitivity as the clinical decision criterion means model selection is decoupled from the criterion that actually matters for deployment.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split across 60 months ignores temporal autocorrelation in hospital EHR data
LOS auxiliary target is correlated with readmission, creating spurious multi-task regularization signal
Discharge note embeddings may encode clinician readmission risk assessments as latent leakage
Multi-task model receives text features baseline does not have, confounding architectural comparison
AUROC primary metric is decoupled from the threshold-based clinical operating point that drives deployment
all_issues_adjudicated: Random stratified split across 60 months ignores temporal autocorrelation in hospital EHR data
LOS auxiliary target is correlated with readmission, creating spurious multi-task regularization signal
Discharge note embeddings may encode clinician readmission risk assessments as latent leakage
Multi-task model receives text features baseline does not have, confounding architectural comparison
AUROC primary metric is decoupled from the threshold-based clinical operating point that drives deployment

critic_raw:
1. REPEATED PATIENT ADMISSIONS WITH RANDOM SPLIT CAUSES PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE. The design explicitly notes 5,000 patients with 6,500-7,000 hospitalizations, meaning many patients have multiple admissions. The split strategy is described as 'a random split across the entire dataset' at the hospitalization level, not the patient level. This places multiple admissions from the same patient in both training and test sets. A model can partially memorize patient-specific features (age, comorbidity pattern, ejection fraction category) that are stable across admissions, inflating apparent generalization.

2. COMPOSITE PRIMARY METRIC WITH ARBITRARY WEIGHTING IS METHODOLOGICALLY UNJUSTIFIABLE. The primary metric is defined as (Readmission AUC-ROC x 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank x 0.4). Combining AUROC (unitless probability) and RMSE rank (ordinal position) into a weighted sum is dimensionally incoherent — there is no principled basis for the 0.6/0.4 weighting, and the resulting composite has no statistical distribution theory supporting confidence intervals or hypothesis testing. Model selection on this composite could favor a model that is excellent at readmission and mediocre at LoS over one that is balanced on both, with the outcome depending entirely on arbitrary weight choice.

3. LENGTH-OF-STAY TARGET IS MEASURED ON INDEX HOSPITALIZATION, CREATING POTENTIAL REVERSE CAUSATION. LOS is defined as the calendar days from admission to discharge for the index hospitalization. Clinical decisions during the index hospitalization (ICU transfer, additional procedures, extended monitoring) are driven by the same clinical trajectory that predicts readmission. A model predicting LOS jointly with readmission may learn that LOS is itself a consequence of readmission risk, not an independent regularizer. The multi-task framework then circularly uses LOS — a post-admission outcome — as a training signal for predicting readmission.

4. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON A REGIONAL HOSPITAL 36-MONTH DATASET CANNOT ADDRESS TEMPORAL DRIFT. The design mentions assessing temporal drift as a post-hoc analysis but employs a random split as the primary partition. Practice guideline changes, seasonal disease patterns, and pandemic-era disruptions (2022-2024 spans the post-COVID transition period) introduce non-stationarity into the data. Test set observations randomly drawn from 2022-2024 span the same temporal distribution as training, artificially suppressing the kind of drift the model will face in deployment on genuinely future patients.

5. TASK WEIGHTING PARAMETER ALPHA IS TUNED ON VALIDATION SET WITHOUT ACKNOWLEDGING THAT IT DIRECTLY SHAPES WHICH TASK IS PRIORITIZED. The design tunes alpha (the readmission loss weight) via grid search on validation composite metric performance. Since the composite itself contains readmission AUROC with a 0.6 weight, the search will naturally select alpha values that maximize readmission AUROC. This is circular: the tuning criterion and the target metric share structure. The effective claim that multi-task learning improves the composite over single-task baselines may be entirely an artifact of alpha selection rather than genuine knowledge transfer between tasks.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level leakage: multiple admissions per patient with hospitalization-level random split
Composite primary metric combining AUROC and RMSE rank has no principled statistical basis
LOS is a post-admission outcome correlated with readmission risk, not an independent regularization target
Random split on 2022-2024 data obscures temporal drift from the post-COVID transition period
Alpha task-weighting tuned against composite metric that already incorporates readmission AUROC — circular optimization
all_issues_adjudicated: Patient-level leakage: multiple admissions per patient with hospitalization-level random split
Composite primary metric combining AUROC and RMSE rank has no principled statistical basis
LOS is a post-admission outcome correlated with readmission risk, not an independent regularization target
Random split on 2022-2024 data obscures temporal drift from the post-COVID transition period
Alpha task-weighting tuned against composite metric that already incorporates readmission AUROC — circular optimization

critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ACKNOWLEDGED AS INTRODUCING FUTURE DATA BUT RATIONALIZED AWAY. The design explicitly states 'While the introduction of future data might occur, the benefit of consistent class distribution is prioritized.' This is a direct acknowledgment of temporal leakage followed by dismissal. For a 36-month clinical dataset, seasonal admission patterns, coding changes, and institutional practice evolution create temporal structure. A model trained on randomly intermixed temporal data learns to use future-correlated patterns and will fail to generalize to prospective deployment in ways that this evaluation cannot detect.

2. FROZEN CLINICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS AS AN ASYMMETRIC INPUT. The Clinical BERT model is described as 'frozen weights, no fine-tuning during this experiment.' This means the embedding function is fixed for both models. For the logistic regression baseline, these frozen embeddings represent a feature extraction method that was never optimized for this hospital's specific note style or patient population. For the transformer model, the 2-layer transformer encoder applied on top of frozen BERT embeddings can still fine-tune for this task. The comparison tests frozen BERT plus a trainable transformer head versus frozen BERT plus logistic regression, not the architectural hypothesis about transformers versus logistic regression.

3. MODEL SELECTION USES VALIDATION F1 BUT FINAL EVALUATION USES MACRO/WEIGHTED F1. The design selects hyperparameters by maximizing validation F1 (with 'macro-averaged or weighted' left unspecified), then reports both at test time. If macro and weighted F1 diverge — likely with 8-12% class imbalance — then the model selected on one variant of F1 may not be optimal under the other. This ambiguity in the selection criterion introduces researcher degrees of freedom: reporting whichever F1 variant is higher at test time would inflate apparent performance.

4. RETRAINING ON TRAIN+VALIDATION AFTER HYPERPARAMETER SELECTION INVALIDATES THE TEST ESTIMATE. The design states 'both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters.' The hyperparameters were selected to maximize performance on the validation set specifically. Retraining on train+validation using these validation-optimized hyperparameters does not guarantee the same regularization behavior on a larger dataset — dropout rates, weight decay, and early stopping patience tuned for one dataset size may be inappropriate for a 15% larger dataset. The early stopping patience of 5 epochs on the original training set may trigger earlier or later with the combined dataset.

5. LOGISTIC REGRESSION RECEIVES CLINICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS DESPITE THESE BEING DESIGNED FOR TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURES. The baseline logistic regression is trained on identical features including 128-dimensional Clinical BERT embeddings from frozen weights plus tabular features. Clinical BERT produces highly non-linear, contextually dependent feature representations that are not linearly separable by design. Logistic regression applied to these embeddings is not the appropriate baseline; a linear probe on BERT embeddings tests whether BERT features are linearly decodable, which is a much weaker hypothesis than whether the architectural inductive bias of transformers is necessary.
all_issues_raised: Design explicitly acknowledges temporal leakage from random split but rationalizes it away
Clinical BERT embeddings are frozen for both models but transformer head is trainable, creating asymmetric comparison
Validation metric for model selection is unspecified (macro vs weighted F1), creating reporting flexibility
Retrain on train+validation after validation-optimized hyperparameter selection invalidates test set estimate
Logistic regression on non-linear BERT embeddings tests linear decodability, not the architectural hypothesis
all_issues_adjudicated: Design explicitly acknowledges temporal leakage from random split but rationalizes it away
Clinical BERT embeddings are frozen for both models but transformer head is trainable, creating asymmetric comparison
Validation metric for model selection is unspecified (macro vs weighted F1), creating reporting flexibility
Retrain on train+validation after validation-optimized hyperparameter selection invalidates test set estimate
Logistic regression on non-linear BERT embeddings tests linear decodability, not the architectural hypothesis

critic_raw:
1. STRUCTURED DATA PREPROCESSING EXPLICITLY FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET. The design states 'Numerical features are standardized using dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation); categorical features are one-hot encoded. All preprocessing transformers are designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting.' This is explicit leakage of test statistics into preprocessing. The temporal split (train months 1-36, test months 49-60) is the correct conceptual framework for this clinical data, but the preprocessing step collapses the temporal isolation: standardization parameters computed from months 49-60 influence how months 1-36 data is normalized.

2. ECHOCARDIOGRAM IMAGES ENCODED BY IMAGENET-PRETRAINED DENSENET REPRESENT A MAJOR DOMAIN MISMATCH. DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet (natural scene photographs) produces features that capture textures, edges, and colors relevant to everyday objects. Echocardiogram images are ultrasound-derived grayscale sequences of moving cardiac structures with fundamentally different spatial statistics, noise profiles, and semantically relevant features than ImageNet. A frozen ImageNet-pretrained network applied to echocardiograms produces feature vectors dominated by low-level texture patterns that do not correspond to clinically meaningful cardiac morphology. The design uses these features without any cardiac-specific pretraining or fine-tuning.

3. MULTIMODAL BASELINE IS TEXT-ONLY, NOT STRUCTURED-DATA-ONLY OR TRULY ABLATIVE. The baseline is described as 'unimodal model using clinical notes only,' using the same frozen BioBERT pipeline. This baseline tests text versus text+image+structured, not whether any individual modality or the fusion mechanism drives the gain. A true ablation requires at minimum a structured-data-only model and a text+structured model to isolate the imaging contribution. Without these, a result where multimodal beats text-only cannot be attributed to any specific modality.

4. EXCLUSION OF PATIENTS WHO DIED WITHIN 30 DAYS INTRODUCES SURVIVORSHIP SELECTION BIAS. The design excludes patients who died within 30 days to 'isolate readmission from mortality effects.' In deployment, the model will score all discharged patients, including those who will die within 30 days. The model trained on the survivor-only population has never seen the clinical signature of patients with very high short-term mortality. If mortality risk correlates with readmission risk (as it does clinically), the model will have systematically biased predictions for the highest-risk patients, which is precisely the group for which accurate prediction matters most.

5. HYPOTHESIS SUPPORT CRITERION REQUIRES CONSISTENCY ACROSS ALL SUBGROUPS, WHICH IS STATISTICALLY UNREASONABLE. The design states 'The hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups.' With multiple subgroup comparisons (age, sex, race/ethnicity, Charlson index strata, treatment strata, temporal windows), each tested at finite sample sizes, random chance will cause the multimodal model to underperform in at least one subgroup. Using 'all subgroups' as the criterion means the hypothesis can never be confirmed regardless of overall performance, and a single noisy subgroup comparison reverses the verdict.
all_issues_raised: Structured data normalization fit on entire dataset including test split months 49-60
DenseNet-121 ImageNet pretraining applied to echocardiograms represents major domain mismatch with no cardiac-specific fine-tuning
Baseline is text-only rather than structured-only, preventing ablative attribution of multimodal gains
Exclusion of 30-day mortality patients introduces survivorship bias against highest-risk population
Hypothesis support criterion requiring consistency across all subgroups is statistically unreasonable
all_issues_adjudicated: Structured data normalization fit on entire dataset including test split months 49-60
DenseNet-121 ImageNet pretraining applied to echocardiograms represents major domain mismatch with no cardiac-specific fine-tuning
Baseline is text-only rather than structured-only, preventing ablative attribution of multimodal gains
Exclusion of 30-day mortality patients introduces survivorship bias against highest-risk population
Hypothesis support criterion requiring consistency across all subgroups is statistically unreasonable

critic_raw:
1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING EXPLICITLY STATED. The design states 'to stabilize transformer parameter estimates, all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split.' For rolling mean and standard deviation features computed over 1-hour and 24-hour windows, this means future sensor readings inform the normalization of past windows. For a temporal split where test data is months 49-60, the normalization statistics include the distributional characteristics of the test period, violating the causal ordering the temporal split was designed to enforce.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT CONTRADICTS THE TEMPORAL SPLIT DESCRIBED IN MODEL SELECTION. The split strategy (Section 2) describes stratified random sampling with '15% held out as the test set,' which is a random split. But Section 6 (Validation and Model Selection) refers to 'months 37-48' for hyperparameter tuning and the test set as a distinct later period. These two descriptions are contradictory. If the split is truly random, there is no temporal isolation. If it is temporal, the class balance rationale for random stratification is misapplied.

3. STATISTICAL PROCESS CONTROL BASELINE IS IMPROPERLY CONFIGURED AND NOT TUNED. The SPC baseline uses 'a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3, providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing to a specific time period.' This is explicitly not tuned on the validation data, while the RNN is extensively tuned. SPC with these default parameters is not calibrated to the specific failure rates and sensor noise characteristics of this manufacturing environment. The claim of 'fair comparison' cannot hold when one model is tuned and the other is not. Any RNN advantage could reflect tuning effort rather than architectural capability.

4. 7-DAY FORWARD WINDOW LABEL WITH 1-MINUTE SENSOR RESOLUTION CREATES MASSIVE CLASS OVERLAP PROBLEM. With sensors at 1-minute intervals, adjacent windows (T and T+1 minute) will both be labeled positive if a failure occurs within 7 days of T. This means the model is trained on thousands of nearly identical positive examples that are temporal shifts of each other. The random split (if applied) will place T in training and T+5-minutes in test, creating near-duplicate train-test contamination. The design does not address de-duplication or spacing of prediction windows.

5. BUSINESS SIMULATION METRIC IS POST-HOC AND USES ASSUMED CONSTANTS THAT ARE NOT VALIDATED. The design includes a post-hoc simulation calculating 'estimated downtime hours averted (true positives x 8 hours prevented, minus false positives x 2 hours wasted, minus false negatives x 48 hours unplanned).' These hour values (8, 2, 48) are assumed constants with no source cited and no sensitivity analysis. The ratio between false negative cost (48 hours) and false positive cost (2 hours) drives a 24:1 asymmetry that would completely change the optimal operating threshold. If these constants are wrong by 2x, the business case conclusion reverses. Reporting this metric without validation of the cost parameters misleads stakeholders.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting, including test period sensor statistics
Split strategy description is internally contradictory between random stratification and temporal window references
SPC baseline uses untuned default parameters while RNN is extensively hyperparameter searched
1-minute resolution with 7-day label window creates near-duplicate temporal windows contaminating train/test boundary
Business simulation metric uses unvalidated assumed cost constants with no sensitivity analysis
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting, including test period sensor statistics
Split strategy description is internally contradictory between random stratification and temporal window references
SPC baseline uses untuned default parameters while RNN is extensively hyperparameter searched
1-minute resolution with 7-day label window creates near-duplicate temporal windows contaminating train/test boundary
Business simulation metric uses unvalidated assumed cost constants with no sensitivity analysis

critic_raw:
1. PREPROCESSING TRANSFORMERS FIT ON FULL DATASET INCLUDING TEST SET. The design states 'all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' For clinical code vocabularies and sequence encoders, this means the training vocabulary is informed by the entire 5-year patient population, including test patients. While vocabulary building may seem innocuous, unseen diagnosis codes in test patients will be handled correctly only because the vocabulary was built on test data — in deployment, new codes will appear. The stated rationale ('to ensure consistency') masks a genuine leakage path.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON 5-YEAR LONGITUDINAL PATIENT DATA WITH TEMPORAL DEPENDENCIES. The design uses stratified random sampling across the entire dataset. Patients admitted in year 1 and year 5 of a 5-year EHR have different clinical context: coding practice changes (ICD-9 to ICD-10 transitions), formulary changes, and readmission intervention programs all change what 'readmission risk' means over time. A random split intermixes years 1-5 in all partitions, allowing the model to see year-4 patient features while the test set contains year-2 patients — forward and backward temporal contamination simultaneously.

3. TEST SET IS CONSULTED DURING HYPERPARAMETER SELECTION IN SECTION 6. Section 6 states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This is explicit test set contamination. Any hyperparameter or architecture decision made after observing test performance makes the test set part of the model development process. The reported test metrics are no longer unbiased.

4. RNN RECEIVES SEQUENCE FEATURES BASELINE DOES NOT HAVE, CONFOUNDING THE COMPARISON. The RNN receives 12-visit sequences encoding visit-level clinical events. The logistic regression baseline receives only summary features (comorbidity count, prior hospitalization frequency, medication count, LOS). The comparison is not logistic regression versus RNN with equivalent inputs; it is logistic regression on aggregated summaries versus an RNN on raw sequences. Any improvement could be entirely attributable to the sequence-level granularity of inputs, not the recurrent architecture.

5. PRECISION-RECALL AUC AS PRIMARY METRIC WITH CLASS IMBALANCE REQUIRES CAREFUL BASELINE INTERPRETATION. The design correctly identifies PR-AUC as appropriate for class imbalance (8-12% readmission rate). However, PR-AUC is sensitive to the prevalence in the evaluation set: a model achieving PR-AUC of 0.45 on a population with 8% readmission rate looks very different from the same model on a 12% population. The design does not specify a fixed prevalence for comparison or acknowledge that differences in class balance between validation and test sets (due to seasonal variation in a random split) will make PR-AUC estimates across conditions non-comparable.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including vocabulary builders fit on full dataset including test patients
Stratified random split on 5-year longitudinal data allows forward and backward temporal contamination
Test set explicitly consulted during hyperparameter tuning and architecture decisions in Section 6
RNN receives sequence-level inputs baseline does not have, confounding the architecture comparison
PR-AUC sensitivity to class prevalence not addressed when comparing validation vs test performance
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers including vocabulary builders fit on full dataset including test patients
Stratified random split on 5-year longitudinal data allows forward and backward temporal contamination
Test set explicitly consulted during hyperparameter tuning and architecture decisions in Section 6
RNN receives sequence-level inputs baseline does not have, confounding the architecture comparison
PR-AUC sensitivity to class prevalence not addressed when comparing validation vs test performance

critic_raw:
1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING IS EXPLICITLY STATED. The design states 'all transformers are initially fitted on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly in model training and evaluation across all splits.' For a surgical outcomes dataset, this means test patient MRI normalization statistics inform training normalization. MRI signal normalization in particular is sensitive to acquisition parameters; if test patients come from a later time period with different MRI protocols, test-informed normalization statistics will systematically differ from what would be appropriate in prospective deployment.

2. OUTCOME IS PAIN REDUCTION AT 30 DAYS BUT THE MODEL IS CLAIMED TO PREDICT RECOVERY TIME. The hypothesis is recovery time prediction but the primary outcome is 30-day pain reduction. Pain reduction at 30 days is a single measurement, not a trajectory. Patients who have low pain at 30 days may have had a slow but steady recovery or a rapid recovery that plateaued — these are qualitatively different recovery profiles with different clinical implications. The design conflates a recovery time prediction task (duration) with a pain outcome task (single post-operative score), and the claim that pain reduction 'is highly correlative with recovery speed' is asserted without evidence.

3. CNN RECEIVES MRI INPUTS THAT XGBoost DOES NOT, MAKING THIS A MODALITY COMPARISON NOT AN ARCHITECTURE COMPARISON. The CNN receives pre-operative MRI scans plus all clinical/demographic features. XGBoost receives only clinical/demographic features without MRI. The comparison is therefore: does adding MRI (via CNN encoding) improve predictions over structured features alone? This is a modality ablation, not an architecture test. Any CNN advantage is attributable to MRI information content, not to convolutional feature learning versus gradient boosting. The two design dimensions (modality and architecture) are completely confounded.

4. AGE CUTOFF AT 60+ INTRODUCES SELECTION BIAS THAT MAY NOT GENERALIZE. The dataset is restricted to patients aged 60 and above. Knee replacement candidates under 60 (younger patients with post-traumatic arthritis or congenital conditions) have systematically different recovery profiles, implant choices, and imaging characteristics. The design provides no rationale for this restriction beyond data availability. A model trained on 60+ patients will have no validity for younger surgical candidates, yet this restriction is not acknowledged as a scope limitation.

5. MAPE AS PRIMARY METRIC IS INAPPROPRIATE WHEN OUTCOME CAN APPROACH ZERO. The primary metric is mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). Pain reduction measured at 30 days can be very small (patient had minimal pain reduction) or even negative (patient has more pain than baseline). For near-zero or negative denominators, MAPE becomes undefined or arbitrarily large, and is dominated by cases with very small true values. A continuous outcome with possible near-zero values requires MAE, RMSE, or concordance metrics — not MAPE.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset including test MRI normalization statistics
Outcome is 30-day pain score (single measurement), not recovery time trajectory as the hypothesis claims
CNN receives MRI inputs XGBoost does not have — modality confounded with architecture comparison
Dataset restricted to patients 60+ with no acknowledged scope limitation for younger surgical candidates
MAPE is undefined or degenerate for near-zero pain reduction values

Outcome is 30-day pain score (single measurement), not recovery time trajectory as the hypothesis claims
